content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Documentaries where a crew uncover something that is bigger than they thought

**Score:** 563
**Body:** Documentaries where a crew dig too deep into something and end up revealing the whole thing

Like if they only knew surface level knowledge and they accidentally discover the whole iceberg.  

Any topic is fine even the darkest ones if there are any. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Keepers is initially about the murder of a Baltimore nun, but as they look into it, they unravel an entire child abuse scandal that went on for years. 
  - I feel like I really need a follow up documentary to that like there were so many unanswered questions still
2. Every episode of How To with John Wilson
3. "three identical strangers"

about identical triplets robert shafran, eddy galland, and david kellman, who were separated at birth and discovered each other by chance at age 19 in 1980. 

their joyous reunion and subsequent media fame lead to the uncovering of a disturbing, secret scientific study that deliberately placed them in different socioeconomic environments to investigate the "nature vs. nurture" debate, revealing a sinister truth behind their separation. 

other sets of twins/triplets separated at birth by the same scientific study were also later identified.
4. Icarus (2017) -- starts with a guy trying PEDs for amateur cycling, and leads to the Russian Olympic doping program
5. Tickled
6. The Unknown Number: The High School Catfish. This kinda falls into this category. It’s jaw-dropping. 
7. The Alabama Solution

Documentary covering a BBQ at an Alabama Prison and Eureka, there is wide spread abuse going on. 

Pretty harsh in your face conditions exposed. 
8. The Jinx
9. Exit Through The Gift Shop from 2010 if you want something that isn’t quite as dark as the more crime focused docs.

## 2. Tremors (1990) is such a fun and highly entertaining movie.

**Score:** 276
**Body:** • Chemistry between all of the cast​ is outstanding. The movie has a lot of funny  moments and dialogues.

• Graboids are genuinely scary, pretty fast , and decently smart too. I like the monsters very much.

• There are also several exhilarating moments, like roof top scene and escape scene.

• I loved the slightly mad couple who literally had fuckton tools and and weapons.

• The soundtrack is also great and movie doesn't leave any wasting space.

**Imo, its a solid 10/10 watch.**  ​

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tremors is a lot of fun. I'm adding that to my rewatch list. Probably haven't seen it for fifteen years? 
2. Some people describe it as a “perfect” movie.

Every scene sets up something that pays off later. No line of dialogue is wasted. Practical effects are stellar. Even the opening foreshadows the climax at the end. Incredibly tight script.

A good example is Chang’s refrigeration pump being broken, and how that pays off later because the main characters hadn’t fixed it yet.

3. What kind of fuse is that?

Cannon fuse.

What the hell do you use it for?

My cannon.
4. When I was a kid sometimes I would start thinking of tremors when I was on my way across the yard or getting out of the car and I would have to make a mad dash for the house. I was so scared lol.
5. I work from home in my basement. A few years ago we had a "powerful for NJ" earthquake. I immediately joked it's the Tremors monsters. We watched 1, 2, and 3 that weekend.
6. We’re not handymen. We’re handy men. RIP Fred Ward.
7. Love. It's a great creature horror that I never hear about
8. The dude‎, Michael Gross, with the weapons was from Family Ties, his character was literally a hippie who had changed with the times, so him being a gun nut goes against type. The wife was famous as well, not sure what for. 
  - Reba McEntire. The queen of country.
9. Saw it in the theater as a teenager. It's a B movie that is actually prestige.

## 3. I‘m having a very bad mental health day. Please recommend movies that make you feel happy

**Score:** 261
**Body:** Technically any genre - either upbeat/ funny movies or movies that make you appreciate the gift of life. Anything that will motivate me to get out of this slump really. Nothing that focuses on romantic love (i.e., no romcoms) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Birdcage is hands down my favorite comfort movie when I’m full spiral 
2. Drop Dead Gorgeous, although it's a dark comedy it's still one of the funniest movies I've ever seen
3. **My Neighbor Totoro (1988)** \- animated fantasy film about childhood wonder

**The Straight Story (1999)** \- uplifting drama where an old man drives 240 miles on a lawnmower to meet his estranged brother
4. Pracilla queen of the desert. No romance just three Aussie queens being their best selves across the continent
5. Napoleon Dynamite is good for days like this imo. The vibe is extremely gentle.
6. Best in show 
7. What We Do In the Shadows 
8. Airplane...
9. Austin Powers, any one of them. Silly fun
10. Little Miss Sunshine 

## 4. Bugonia – worth a watch

**Score:** 206
**Body:** Just watched Bugonia and honestly didn’t expect much but it turned out to be really good. Definitely worth a watch.

Emma Stone was great in this, felt very natural. Also read that she actually shaved her head for the role, which is pretty wild and adds to the character. Jesse Plemons was really good too, solid performance from him.

What I liked most is that it doesn’t drag. It kind of quietly keeps you hooked the whole time, no unnecessary scenes or filler. And the twist towards the end was actually solid, didn’t see it coming.

Overall, a really pleasant surprise for me. Would recommend 👍

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I see absolutely no reason why Jesse Plemons was not nominated for best actor for his role in this movie. He did phenomenal.
  - Not only nominated but should've won. 
  - he had the best performance of the year IMO. should have been nominated and should have won
2. Why would you not expect much from Yorgos?
  - Yeah, this review weirdly treats the movie like it wasn't one of the most critically acclaimed, talked about movies of the year, and like its director and lead actress isn't coming off a 2023 movie that won 4 Oscars.


"Wasn't expecting much but pleasant surprise worth a watch" would be how I'd describe Josh Hartnett's silly airplane action movie *Fight or Flight* from 2025.


Anyway, *Bugonia* was the best movie I saw last year.
3. Talking about "the twist towards the end" is a spoiler. Think about it.
  - I second this. Anyone saying “twist” ruins a movies surprise. 
4. Ok, i'm sold, putting it on now! Thanks 😊 
5. The original is worth a watch if you enjoyed the remake.  
6. I loved that movie and then watch the director’s entire collection. Top notch crazy in the best way.

## 5. Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die, is such an underrated movie. It's one of the best movies I've seen in a long while for what I thought was a joke movie.

**Score:** 149
**Body:** Honestly an incredible movie from the start to the plot twist at the end. Loved it. Not for everyone but a definite shame for how much it flopped.





### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved this movie. I really wish it was shown more nationally.
2. I saw it as well and thoroughly enjoyed it.
3. Fantastic movie with fantastic twists. I’ve been telling everyone about it 
4. This movie totally had me until the last 20 mins which I still enjoyed but I just didn’t like it as much as I had until that point. It’s also what stopped me from recommending it to others. 
5. Quick question. Would someone who loved Everything Everywhere All At Once find similar aspects in this movie to love? I ask because from what i’ve seen/heard it kind of gives me that vibe. I’ll probably see it regardless because I really like Sam Rockwell but just wanted to know
  - Really fun movie. 

Not really the same vibe as eeaao. To me anyway. 
  - I can’t stand Everything everywhere all at once and I thought Good Luck, Have Fun, Don’t Die is one of the best movies in the last 5 years.

Everything is an absurdist sci fi multiverse movie.  It’s chaotic and weird.

Good luck is grounded in real world thinking about the future of humanity.  It’s serious and reflective 
6. I loved it. 
7. Incredible film. Fantastic cinematography! 
8. So good! Sam Rockwell in the opening scene alone was worth the price of admission!

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. Which Nepo baby actors groomed to be the next big stars bombed the hardest?

**Score:** 23678
**Body:** Big publicity and push for Jaden Smith in the early 10's. After Earth was the final nail in the coffin of his acting career 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Key and Peele skit showing Jayden looking for new offers is hilarious.

He still has one fan
2. [deleted]
3. Tori Spelling

4. Scott Eastwood seemingly leapfrogged straight into a major supporting role in the Fast and Furious franchise and leading man roles in romance movies, and the public just did. not. bite.
5. Rumer Willis 
Edit. Spelling

![gif](giphy|3rgXBBYCVzihkifBRK)
  - OLD MAAAAN RIIIIVER

## 2. Jason Isaacs in The Patriot is one of the most despicable movie villains ever

**Score:** 15927
**Body:** Rewatched The Patriot and I cannot believe how much I hate his character.

Just merciless and a tyrant. Jason Isaacs absolutely crushed it in the role.

Without a doubt, he’s gotta be one of the most hated villains of all time. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tell me about… *Ohio.*
  - Only Jason Isaacs could deliver a line like that and make it sound menacing. 
  - Every Michigander hears that line and thinks,

"Oh fucking wow, he really is fucking terrible!"
2. Agreed. That guy is great in everything he's been in. 
  - He absolutely needs to be a Bond villain. 
  - https://i.redd.it/88xbv6clcqng1.gif


3. Yeah he was great. My buddies and I saw it in theaters. Absolutely hated his character. He was also great in death of Stalin as general Zhukov. 
Edit :not stukov that was the StarCraft general. Haha my bad. 
4. Stupid boy.
5. https://i.redd.it/oom2xtgdspng1.gif
6. He is horrid. Jason Isaacs has some of the most piercing eyes and in this movie you can just tell he has ill intentions from his gaze. He is so cruel. His death is so gratifying. I love this movie.

## 3. This is the reunion we've been waiting for

**Score:** 10301

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cindy, the TV's leaking - Still lives rent free in my head
  - "This bitch is messing up my floors" 
    - I love in the first (third*) one when Cindy is talking to ghost face (brenda*) and he says “I know you did last summer” 

“It was Mardi Gras and I was out of beads!!”

Edit: oops. Had the movie wrong.
    - "Oh my god, Brenda! We're gonna die"
"Well, it just would've been you if you would've just shut the fuck up!"
  - I love "You know your teacher Mrs. Brenda?"

"Yeah-"

"SHE'S DEAD! GONE FOREVER! DIED A HORRIBLE, PAINFUL DEATH! GONE, GONE, GONE JUST LIKE YOUR DOG!"

"My dog's dead?!"

"I JUST RAN HIM OVER WITH THE CAR WHEN I DROVE IN! EVERYONE YOU LOVE AROUND YOU'S DYING!"
2. And we don't question Brenda's immortality 
  - I was going to say, it's been a while since I've seen the movies but hasn't she died at least twice? 
3. goddesses. sashimi. Toyota. Mitsubishi
4. Brenda is the funniest in these movies.
5. The screenshot made me realize I'm still not over my crush on Anna Faris

## 4. Jack o connell gave the best acting performance in Sinners imo. Crazy how he wasn't even nominated for the oscars .

**Score:** 8087

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Imagine if he had been, and then been the only award for Sinners.  The white guy in a black story.
  - But he was playing an irish guy. 
2. UK version of Skins has produced so many fantastic actors. 
  - "uk version"

I didn't even know there was a different skins 
    - The UK Skins is the original. 
3. If you haven't seen it, he is phenomenal in Starred Up.
  - And 28 Years Later - Bone Temple. 
    - Howzat
      - And god did nothing, for he was invisible… and cannae punch his way out of a paper fucking bag anyway.

## 5. This movie is absolute chaos, and the misunderstanding-based humor makes it so entertaining. What do you think about Tucker and Dale vs. Evil?

**Score:** 7974
**Body:** I genuinely believe it’s a hidden gem, and it gave me one of the most fun experiences I’ve had watching a movie. There were zero dull moments; it was fun from start to finish.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “You must think I’m some kind of moron to believe a story like that.”

“No, no sir not at all just….open-minded?”
  - I love that in their conversation Tucker says it would be ridiculous to say this to a cop, then the cop arrives and this is basically line for line what he tells him
    - We have had a REAL DOOZY of a day!
2. I love when the kid runs into the wood chipper and the guy is like “You okay?!”
  - He’s heavy for half a guy. THE STORE HAD BETTER NOT CHARGE ME FOR THIS.
3. WE GOT YOUR FRIEND 
  - OH MY GOD THEY HAVE SARAH!
4. https://i.redd.it/6qd14qexznlg1.gif
5. It’s a masterpiece.
6. Whenever people ask for a good Horror Comedy.

This is usually my recommendation.

So good, Alan Tudyk is amazing as always.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. Sinners doesn’t make a strong enough case for why Vampirism is a bad thing

**Score:** 781
**Body:** I’ve watched Sinners 4 times, and though I find it to be a fascinating watch, I’ve pinned down one of my biggest critiques for this movie: the lack of a strong argument presented for why becoming a Vampire would be a bad thing. I appreciate the movie’s willingness to present the vampirism as a complex trade, but in doing so, it goes too far in the opposite direction, to the point where it becomes a bit of a head scratcher why the human characters are so resistant to the idea.

The biggest example of this is the end credits scene, where we see Stack as a Vampire decades following the events of the scene. His personality is still incredibly similar to how it was pre-Vampirism, and he gets to live for as long as he wants with the woman he loves. From where I’m sitting, that doesn’t really seem like a bad bargain. Yes, the movie vaguely gestures to the idea of Vampirism robbing an individual of their culture, and while that’s a solid case to make, it doesn’t strongly engage with this downside or show what form this actually takes, outside of one scene where the black Americans are assimilated into singing an Irish song, which in itself was framed in a very visually appealing manner.

All in all, I wish that the movie made a stronger case against the Vampirism: in doing so, it would’ve made the philosophical questions of the movie more compelling. As it is, the Vampirism doesn’t seem like a bad trade for me, as the characters get to keep their personalities and spend an eternity with their loved ones.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree that the trade-offs between non-vampire and vampire aren't the most clearly articulated. (The vampires maybe don't entirely lose their culture, but they do seem to lose a certain degree of their individuality.) But instead of this being to the movie's detriment, I think that's what makes it so interesting, and keeps you thinking about it longer than if it were a more black-and-white choice. If becoming a vampire in Sinners is a metaphor for cultural assimilation, it makes a case that there are some upsides to assimilation, whether you're ultimately in favor of it or not.

I'll put it this way: If the movie was more firmly against the concept of becoming a vampire, maybe the messaging would have been a bit cleaner, but you might not then have spent so much time thinking about it that you eventually made this whole post trying to figure out what it's trying to say. And I probably wouldn't have gone through the trouble of writing this response. The grayness in the choice is what makes it more interesting to think and talk about.
  - I think the movie doesn’t intend to oppose the concept of ‘vampirism’ just like it doesn’t oppose the concept of Christianity: Sammy can still hold on to the teachings of a Christian faith, for instance, while not following the strict worldview from his father’s church at that time. Cultural blends impact on theological affiliations. Christianity wasn't part of the belief system of Sammy's ancestors. But it is and forever will be part of who he is. 

At the end, he releases himself from this particular view of the religion. With the vampires, they seem to work as a hive mind, so Remmick's death also gives the surviving vampire couple a way to exist as vampires without this overpowered, controlling figure as their master dictating all their actions.
    - I thought about this too, especially since in a lot of vampire movie tropes you’ve got, like, Renfield figures begging to become vampires and get turned and stuff.  Or maybe that was what we do in the shadows, anyway you get my drift.

My short simple answer to this - and now that I’m thinking about it works itself into a kind of a nice analogy - was that because when you became a vampire, you were controlled by Remmick. Everyone was a mono-Borg, controlled by a charismatic but evil white man.

I could see an analogy where, if you take what you can from the vampire/white man, but escape his control, that’s the best outcome?   There’s gotta be an analogy to racial politics in there somewhere.
      - It seems to me that this hive mind logic subjects all new vampires to Remmick's killer thirst, but I also think back to Stack was allowed to live by his brother and didn't go on killing others: somehow, he had some sort of control and free-will even then; I see this as a parallel to Sammy gathering the strength to turn his back on his father's church the next morning. 
  - >If becoming a vampire in Sinners is a metaphor for cultural assimilation, it makes a case that there are some upsides to assimilation, whether you're ultimately in favor of it or not.

This is where I eventually landed as well.

I think the most coherent reading is that the prospect of assimilation is complicated and offers pros and cons that are difficult to weigh. Ultimately each brother chooses opposing sides of the proposition.

To assimilate offers freedom, equality, *and* cultural exchange and connections that are in fact not evil and can be deeply rewarding.

However, to assimilate requires giving up something intangible, ineffable even, which is the deep connection to an ancient, unique past and the ancestors who inhabited it.

I think, ultimately, the film suggests that assimilation is necessary to survive, to continue to exist in the modern world. But that the trade off underlies something of the deep existential dissatisfaction of modern life and in some ways continued connection to the ancient past even through death is preferable. The film I think is somewhat ambivalent about which choice is superior, and I think that is as it should be.

In spite of all this, I think a lot of readings I see that go really hard on Remmick being a "culture vulture" are doing the film a disservice and promoting a questionable message that suggests each culture should remain in its box or else become impure.
2. In the context of the movie, heaven and the afterlife are shown to be real. Being a vampire not only allows you to see beyond the veil of the physical world and gives you supernatural knowledge, but it also locks you out of this afterlife and spiritual continuity. Imagine knowing the pain of realizing all of your ancestors and dead family are together in paradise and knowing you will never, ever join them.

Even with the idea of limited immortality you are sacrificing eternity in paradise for a living hell of being hunted, not seeing the sun, feeling the pain of every other vampire in your hive, etc. It also seems like despite having your personality intact, being a vampire does make your free will questionable.
  - There’s an aspect of Bram Stoker’s Dracula that I’ve never seen reflected in adaptions that would’ve been especially effective here. When vampires are killed in his book, for a moment after their death and before they fade into dust, they look beautiful and peaceful. Because he makes it incredibly clear that their souls are trapped, they have knowledge of what they are but no real control over what they’re doing. Killing them truly is the greatest mercy you can offer.
3. It’s a metaphor for forced integration. The papering over of culture, individuality, and the self. The implication being that Remmick himself was the victim of Christianity and imperialism who then would use his powers to take over their experience. The same argument is also being made in Pluribus; forcing a communal mind would lead to no crime, no racism, no violence… and also no art and no culture. In Sinners, however, it’s a hierarchical system since it’s critiquing American culture via the black experience. African Americans have a shared heritage and history in this country that is divorced from their ancestral heritage, and that shared struggle is why they feel free in a place like the Juke Joint where it is by them and for them. There’s the question of mixed race inclusion with Hailee Stenfield’s character; “why can’t we all be family?” Because he’s not trying to be their family, he’s trying to be their master. It’s not really about the vampirism.
4. That is because the vampirism is a metaphor and not a curse like in the generalized mythology of fantasy. It robs the infected of their authenticity, that link with who their people were. It makes them part of a new conglomerate that seeks to assimilate everyone.
  - I think that, more than robbing one’s authenticity, it strips them of their free-will: everyone becomes ‘property’ of their maker, and their personalities, while still there, are overruled by the philosophy and wants of the ruler (in this case, Remmick). Stack and Mary, for instance, live another day and are released from their master’s murdering ways – are free to follow their own path. Before Remmick is defeat, I believe Stack also displays an unusual self-control for one in the vampire clan when her brother allows him to live. So, Stack is showing that spark of individuality that, for Sammy, comes with turning his back on his father's church the next day.

## 2. Why do modern films look so visually bad?

**Score:** 591
**Body:** This is a problem I’ve had with a lot of films for a few years now, but after watching today’s Spider Man trailer I feel like something is seriously off with the way modern movies are shot and lit. Why does a huge, expensive blockbuster like Spider Man sometimes look like it was filmed in someone’s basement?

I genuinely love movies so it’s frustrating seeing so many of them end up with this grey, washed out, almost sludgy look. The composition is always so awful. What bothers me even more is how many people seem to think it actually looks good.

I’m not trying to start an argument here, I’m honestly just curious. Why do modern movies look like this? Did.  cinematographers and lighting crews just forget how to do their jobs or is something else going on?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hugely expensive movies made by committe have no visual identity and their lack of planning and vision means everything comes out a gray wash. They can't decide on weapons or costumes so it all has to be done in post. Then it becomes a game of having to hide so much CGI, leading to the dim look of a lot of these. The vfx teams are victims of no planning or vision as well, they're forced to churn out version after version until they land on something before some executive changes their minds again. 

Then look at Dune Part 2. An expensive movie that looks expensive, helmed by a director with vision and the capability to plan and execute said vision because he knows what he wants.
  - Movie studios are essentially “Private Equitying” themselves nowadays. They don’t really care about blowing away audiences as much as maintaining the bottom line and keeping stakeholders happy.

They think Becuase we have 3D, green screen and cheaper equipment we can do anything we want for less money. Also, sadly studios think good marketing is all that’s needed to engineer excitement versus actually making something good.

Back in the day even cheesy movies like Transformers actually flew their cast and crew out to the real locations of the Great Pyramids, Scotland, shot on the streets of Thailand and truly made audiences feel as if they were being whisked away to offered parts of the world.

But the truth is I don’t believe audiences have actually ever accepted heavy CG as acceptable. And I think we are seeing more and more of audiences realizing when their brains see it, it really doesn’t register like practical effects once did.

My prediction has been that soon we are going to see a huge return to practical effects.
  - Then look at Dune Part 2

Yeah, this is a great example compared to your average super hero flick these days. It's the difference between having a very intentional vision in your shots/style... compared to a marvel flick where they light everything flat because they know they'll just edit it for a certain look in post. 

Lighting in itself is nearly a lost art in film these days.
2. The digitial sludge has been an issue that has been discussesd and debated for years. There isn't one reason, but several as to why this particular look has been prevalent.  
  
In some cases it's the nature of digital recording which smooths out the image and gives more latitude to tweak the image. Then we have an explosion of different types of screens to watch films on which all have their different set of color standards. Next we have an industry that often copies the latest trend, and this is the current trend. Muted browns and greys are viewed as more realistic, which is what audiences clamor for. Younger generations want the "vibe". 

These are all professionals so it has nothing to do with competence. This is a choice that is being made during pre-production. By the way, when you mention composition that has nothing to do with color.

You can see subtle changes watching films from specific decades to see what were the latest trends and stylistic options cinematographers were choosing from. Hopefully this current trend will end soon. Not all films choose the digital sludge style as evident by a couple of best picture nominees this year.
  - I wish I knew more about this. All I know is I want movies to look more like Anora and The Secret Agent. 

As a novice on the technology, but as a film buff that can see what is happening on the screen, it does baffle me how people enjoy watching some of these movies that just look bland. 
3. There's a lot of pressure to film movies in a way that leaves as much room as possible for changes in post-production. The raw digital footage is extremely grey and dull, but that allows for a huge range of editing options (colour correction and lighting in particular). Flat, soft lighting also makes it much easier to edit in new footage, or move footage around, as all the scenes will look similar.

In theory, the flexibility means you can do more with the footage, but often it just means that the hard work of blocking and lighting on-set gets kicked into the editing room, and then the work doesn't really get done there either.
4. Did you watch any of the Best Picture nominees from last year? Many of them are shot gorgeously. Good movies are definitely still being made, but you have to get out of the Marvel and blockbuster bubble. Check out Bugonia - it has Kubrick vibes. Check out Hamnet, Sinners, Train Dreams, F1, and Frankenstein
  - OBAA looked fantastic.
5. >Why does a huge, expensive blockbuster like Spider Man sometimes look like it was filmed in someone’s basement?

It doesn't. It looks like it's a huge, expensive blockbuster that has had all of its edges filed off by going through seven million levels of approval and does a lot of mid shit to get okay looking CGI at the cheapest price point possible that is still ridiculously expensive.
6. Here’s one pretty good breakdown of some reasons why: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=tvwPKBXEOKE

But it’s kind of become a genre of article and video now so there are plenty of other discussions about it online. There are a variety of factors contributing to this, and different people have tackled different ones. 

## 3. A part of the ending to One Battle After Another that didn't sit right with me: the iPhone

**Score:** 472
**Body:** In general I like the movie, but PTA has mentioned how he has been writing this story for 20+ years and that was one aspect which felt very dated to me. 

Part of the epilogue of the film shows Bob at home with Willa, now playing with a new iPhone. She tries to show him how to take a selfie, he can't quite get it right, but at least he's no longer petrified of the modern world. It's all very cute. 

I think my problem with this part is that I feel like PTA is still treating smartphones the way you would treat a cell phone in a thriller from 1999. They're a gadget. The most nefarious thing they can do is be used to figure out your location. But in 2026, it's a post-Snowden, Cambridge Analytica world, and today not only do we know that his paranoia and fears of surveillance are entirely justified, the government and corporations ARE monitoring every single thing you do, tracking you location, reading all your messages etc. But they are also effectively brainwashing people with algorithms and deliberately driving people into bubbles of fear, hatred and delusion. For corporate profit, for political gain, whatever (and tbh someone like Bob is exactly the kind of guy you can imagine going absolutely fucking nuts on facebook or twitter and sharing AI videos of Bill Clinton eating a baby, but that's besides the point)

Compare OBAA to Eddington and how it depicts the state of modern American life & politics, and specifically smartphones. In general I think the film has its finger on the pulse of current events much more than OBAA. In Eddington, all these people living in their own bubbles, doomscrolling all night, recording themselves killing people - the smartphone is an instrument of disconnecting from reality and the world and making the characters into unreachable loons, whereas OBAA uses it to represent a guy letting go of fear and embracing the modern world. The former feels much more appropriate to me. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's really not that complicated. Now that Lockjaw is dead, Bob & Willa aren't in danger. They're a bunch of nobodies. So they don't have to be as obsessive about technology. This also shows that Bob is trying to change as a person.
2. I think it’s a reasonable observation, but for me it doesn’t affect the quality of either film.  Not every movie set in a modern era has to tackle every issue at the same time.  To keep the story focused requires leaving some issues on the cutting floor.

OBAA takes place in its own universe from my perspective, it’s similar to our own but not 1:1.
  - That’s how I felt, like almost-but-not-quite our world. An adjacent reality.
    - Much like the novel it was based on. 
  - The alternate universe thing is how I took it and how the discourse seems to miss? I presumed the film opens in the 2010s and then skips to 2025ish. However I saw a lot of comments from people thinking it started now then skipped to the 2030s and thought it didn't look "futuristic" enough
    - To me, I thought the botched bank heist car chase clearly showed it was in the near past due to all the old 2000s cars. 
      - Yeah I assumed while watching that the opening was roughly in the Occupy/Bank Crash era. 2008-2011 somewhere.
3. Notice how Sensei and his "underground railroad" all use cell phones and STILL are able to be effective activists? And even without cell phones, almost all of the French 75 still got captured/killed. I think PTA's point is less that technology isn't dangerous, and more that we shouldn't be walling ourselves off from the very communities we're trying to help.
4. Eddington is a movie about how the powers that be manipulate people through social media.

OBAA is not a movie about social media.

Immigration is a pretty big issue right now. You could change your thesis with one word to flip it:

“Compare OBAA to Eddington and how it depicts the state of modern American life & politics, and specifically Immigration.”

5. It’s not a movie about technology.  PTA uses technology as a tool to illustrate the bond between a father and daughter.

OBAA touches on multiple themes and illustrates many issues, but it is in many ways a story about a father and a daughter.

A “father-daughter epic” if you will, about what it means to raise a kid as a solo parent and let them free into the world, and how that process with Willa “healed” and led to growth from Bob.

The phone at the end symbolizes that bond, his growth and her role in helping him get there.

## 4. The 'controversy' over 'Three Billboards Outside of Ebbington, Missouri' is because of people taking things too literally

**Score:** 455
**Body:** Spoilers, obvs, for a film that came out 9 years ago. 



I'll be honest, literalism is my biggest gripe over the last 15 years about films across the board. But this film I actually stayed away from because I heard it basically redeemed a really racist, horrible cop character, just to make the audience (esp white audience) feel good about themselves. That made it sound trite and boring to me. 

But here I was with a sick day saturday to myself, and it was on a streamer, so i watched it. 

It was one of the most devastating portraits of grief and anger that I've ever seen. It reminded me of Blue Ruin and Revanche to name a couple others. Mildred is sort of your hero at the start--who wouldn't feel bad and want to root for a grieving mother?--but as the film goes on you see how her grief has turned her into someone that hurts others a lot, including her surviving son. I wasn't 'rooting' for her in the way I root for like, the main character in Get Out to escape the racist cannibals. I felt for her, and hoped she would find peace. 

Then there's Sam Rockwell's Jason Dixon. Just a complete scumbag, who isn't even good at his job. It's not like he's a great detective, he is just also racist and violent. No, he's kind of a terrible cop, too. 

This is where, though, I was waiting for this cheesy redemption arc. And it never came. 

Yes, he tries to solve the murder, but... he fails? and he does it in a boneheaded, stupid way? 

Then he and Mildred set out to murder that soldier who also busted up Mildred's store earlier. And yeah, maybe he raped and burned a girl while serving overseas but... we don't know that, they don't know that. Even he did, though: wasn't the whole thing we went through in 2020 about restorative justice, abolition of prisoners, and defunding the police, about how cops shouldn't be the judge jury and executioner? 

And if you don't think Martin McDonagh, the filmmaker KNEW this, then you haven't read or seen his other works. McDonagh is the anti-Tarantino. He makes violent movies and plays to show how freaking futile it is to spend your life on revenge. Just like the teenage bride says (from her bookmark about 'polio or polo' lol) anger begets greater anger. It doesn't mean Mildred is 'bad', no, a great film shouldn't be caught up in labeling someone good or bad, so the audience can pat themselves on the back for being 'one of the good ones'. It certainly doesn't make Dixon 'good', cuz he definitely isn't! I'd argue that going to Idaho to murder a guy is just more of the same for him! 

A redemptive arc for Dixon would be, like, going to therapy, and then becoming a therapist himself or something. Not committing vigilante justice. 

So this film was incredible for me, and it wasn't always fun to watch, because of how tragic it was. But I'm glad I finally saw it. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I didn’t love this movie (it was okay) but I always thought this argument against it was so weak. We were not supposed to walk away from it thinking Sam Rockwells character was a hero or redeemed or anything like that. I question the critical thinking skills of anyone who came away from the movie thinking that was what it was trying to make them think. 
  - Yea, the whole point is learning to live together in the shadow of problems you are incapable of fixing. Rockwell’s character ends the story still an awful, violent, crooked cop. The world is full of people like him, and you can’t wish them away. But sometimes, you can come to an understanding with them.

It’s a bit of a shame for me, because it’s got a fantastic premise and themes, an A+ cast, a lot of powerful moments, and so many interesting ideas.  I think it just got let down by the details.
2. Luckily I wrote something about this in 2018, so I can accurately remember what I thought about a movie I haven't seen since then. :)

Most of the complaints seem to center around the character of Dixon and whether he is "redeemed" at the end. I think this criticism not only misses the point of the ending but reveals that the critics of this point might be unaware of what really offends them about it.

The movie isn't about Dixon and his arc, it's about Mildred and her arc. She begins as a righteously angry grieving mother fighting 'the man' with a bold public move, and ends in a morally dubious plan, partnering with a violent racist creep. In this way, the movie can be read as a scathing critique of the Justice Warrior and a warning about how easy it is for self-righteously blinding rage to morph into something unsavory, counter-productive and quite the opposite of how it was intended. I feel like what many of the critics of the politics of the movie are reacting to is how it reflects on themselves, "the good guys," rather than how it muddies its portrait of "the bad guys."  
  
At the end, with rage leading the line, the difference between "good guys" and "bad guys" is neglible.
  - > The movie isn't about Dixon and his arc, it's about Mildred and her arc.

I think it is both, but we only really realize it when going back to re-watch and clearly see their starting and ending points.

Otherwise I very much agree with the rest of your write-up. Mildred starts the story in a very morally defensible and sympathetic situation and ends up making violent and angry choices. And because of that, her end point intersects with the end point of a violent and hateful cop who ends up making choices to be a bit more patient and understanding.  And we see Dixon at his lowest dealing with grief by attacking Red at the advertising office and Mildred at her lowest dealing with grief by burning down the police station. But then Dixon improves and Mildred gets worse following their low points, culminating in their meeting at the middle where neither are 'good'. 

Dixon's arc is interesting because early on you aren't expecting that by the end of the movie you are hoping for him to succeed as he makes better choices (gathering evidence at the bar) and Mildred's is interesting because her cause is so righteous that you get a bit blindsided by how counterproductive her decisions become.


We do also get some examples of 'good' choices from Red, the new chief, and Peter Dinklage's character, and 'bad' choices from Mildred's ex husband and the soldier. So we see the contrasting trajectories of Dixon and Mildred in those contexts too so that we are reminded that they are both at all times capable of deciding the kind of person they want to be.
3. The moral panic around the film and Rockwell’s character was so overblown and overwrought that it truly felt like satire at the time for anyone with even a half-working brain lmao

I cannot fathom how you could watch that film, let alone understand Mcdonagh as a writer/filmmaker, and come away with the idea that he is glorying or supporting the actions of an abusive police system.

It was just low-information viewers hopping on the nebulous concept of “police bad” to pat themselves on the back about their sociopolitical anxieties at the time.

Three Billboards was and is a fantastic film in the catalogue of a fantastic filmmaker.
4. Three Billboards…is a movie that I’m glad that I saw, parts are deeply moving and stayed with me for a long time. But McDonagh doesn’t know a damn thing about American life and it hurts the film; the characters and situations don’t have the cultural specificity of his works set in Ireland.

I’m thinking of the Black friend and employee of Frances McDormand’s character. She gets arrested on a drug possession charge to intimidate Mildred, then, after several days, gets released and happily returns to work, a smile on her face. It’s not really a character at all, just someone who exists in the film to be the object of another character’s racism.
  - The dialogue doesn’t fit either. It makes sense for In Bruges but is jarring and out of place for this setting
5. The thing with controversies like this is they become oversimplified over time. I think there are absolutely issues with how he wrote about racism in this film (in large part because it's something the film focuses a lot on, but primarily just to add texture to characters/setting), but to boil it down to "the movie is bad because it's about a racist cop's redemption" is beyond silly. I think to really explore this topic you'd have to read some critical essays exploring this, and not just go off of the impressions you got off social media. Honestly it kind of reminds me of the One Battle After Another discourse (a movie that I much prefer but is also not beyond criticism).
6. I always thought the film made it very clear that Sam Rockwell's character wasn't redeemed by the end of the film. But he might have started on the road to redemption. Personally I think on the road, the two characters would get sidetracked, and eventually decide against committing vigilante justice. They're getting to the place they need to be but they aren't there yet.
7. My interpretation: that soldier was absolutely the murderer. The whole movie has themes about people overlooking the flaws of their own people, and taking steps to protect their people regardless of right/wrong or legality. So the army officer guy claiming his solider was overseas when the cops call asking is just one more example of this theme. He would know what his soldier was capable of, so naturally he wouldn’t hand his guy over. 

We already know the soldier had a sure connection to Mildred and the daughter’s story, otherwise why’d he come to the store and wreck stuff?

I have zero doubt at all that the soldier did it, and I don’t think it was even ambiguous. The only question in the end is whether Mildred and Dixon will go through with killing the soldier. And I think they do.

The movies not about redemption, it’s about human tribalism, and Dixon has found his new tribe. Just my own interpretation. Personally I loved it.

## 5. I just finished Mulholland Drive and genuinely have no idea what I just watched

**Score:** 369
**Body:** I finally watched Mulholland Drive last night because it’s always on those “greatest movies ever made” lists. I went in expecting a complicated mystery… but I genuinely feel like my brain got scrambled.

For the first hour or so, I thought I understood what was happening. Then the story just kept getting stranger and stranger, and by the end I wasn’t even sure what parts were real, what parts were dreams, or if any of it actually happened.

Some things that completely lost me:

The whole shift in identities near the end

The blue box / blue key situation

Club Silencio (which felt important but I have no idea why)

Whether the first half of the movie is supposed to be a dream

I’m not even saying it’s bad, it was actually really fascinating and creepy but I feel like I’m missing something huge.

So for people who understand this movie…

What is actually going on?

Is there a generally accepted interpretation, or is it intentionally meant to be confusing?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Someone could probably explain this a lot better than me but I'll give it a quick go from what I remember:

Diane (Naomi Watts) and Camilla (Laura Harring) are aspiring actresses who had a sexual relationship with each other that meant a lot more to Diane than it did to Camilla. Eventually Camilla started to become successful and she essentially dropped Diane for the affections of Adam (Justin Theroux), a famous director. Diane became depressed and jealous and hired the hitman to kill Camilla, who told her that the job would be confirmed once she received a blue key. Upon seeing the key, she became guilty with what she had done and was driven to suicide.

Now, that's all covered in the second half of the movie, but the first half is the dream that Diane has in her dying moments, in which all of the details of her reality are moved around to create a more pleasant picture of her life in review. In this world, she's a much more charming and promising young actress named Betty who kills it in her audition and secures the undivided love of a clueless Camilla (aka Rita). Also, the arrogant Adam is a cuck, the hitman can't get his job done, and Diane Selwyn is just a random woman whose dead body they come across, eliminating all of her other real-life concerns. Some of the other details of the dream are more mysterious and unresolved, like the dream of the man in the diner and club silencio, but they can be seen as abstract hints to the lies and ugliness that Diane is  keeping from herself in this state.

In her dream, nasty truths like the meaning of the blue key are turned into fun little plot devices that you would see in a traditional mystery movie, while her record of being cheated on, traumatized, and eventually dying an unglorious death are projected onto other people. Essentially it helps her to have the classic yet unattainable "Hollywood Dream" that she had always wanted just before she dies, simply by being able to shift some stuff around.
  - Agree with all this except that the dream does not take place during her dying moments. At the start we see a POV of a head hitting a pillow, that’s Diane in a depressed state going to sleep. When the Cowboy says ‘time to wake up’ and we hear knocking sounds, that’s when Diane wakes up, because in reality her lesbian ex roommate is knocking on her front door.

She spends the day moping around her house, wracked with depression and guilt over murdering Camilla, and at the end she goes completely insane and blows her brains out.
    - Yeah, the first two hours are the dream (lol the pillow shot) and the last half hour explains the reality. It’s kind of like a reverse Wizard of Oz.
  - You nailed pretty much everything, just that the dream doesn't start in her dying moments but right before it. After the beginning swing dance scene it cuts to a blurry pov shot of someone in bed turning their head into the pillow and fading to black, which is Diane going to sleep and the fantasy dream starting. Then at the end when she wakes up is when she finds the key delivered to her on the table, starts fantasising about their relationship and sex, gets overcome with guilt which then manifests in that old couple chasing her before killing herself. 

And I think Diane Selwyn's corpse in the dream, since that's her name in reality, symbolises both her guilt and her impending doom because of it. As well as a kind of omen or foreshadowing that the dream is coming to an end due to her 'skeletons in the closet'. Which is also a similar thing I think the homeless man jump scare represents—the filthy, repressed guilt.

It's been a while but I think those are the sequence of events. Also, I had this thought just the other day about the Club Silencio scene—there's a lot of meta and surreal stuff going on with illusions and seeing through them. Perhaps it was a real event they both went to, and the 'NO HAY BANDA' performance helped Diane realise the illusion of her romanticised 'Hollywood dream' relationship with Camilla. And this is why she seizures and snaps out of the dream when reexperiencing it.
2. Another thing i would like to add is that for me Mulholland Drive feels like a sister movie to Lost Highway. Both have similar "dissociated state" like structure and similar themes, but from different perspectives. 
  - i'm happy to see somebody else saying this, it's something i been thinking about for like twenty years. two different treatments of the same idea of how do you live with yourself after doing something terrible to somebody, you either invent an entire fantasy world to live in until it falls apart around you, or you physically become a different person
3. I think approaching David Lynch films with "whodunnit" approach is kinda wrong approach to take. Sure, there are some quite plausible solving-the-puzzle explanations you can find online, but IMHO that's not the point of his films. Enjoying magical experience, subconscious mindfucks and having personal explanation is much more worthy and meaningful then having "what is actually going on" explained to you.
  - I watched this film for the first time when i was about 18 and never had an experience like it before. I was amazed at how i could enjoy a film so much and yet have no idea what was going on, i felt something subliminally.

But i immediately went to the internet to look up explainations and honestly that helped me appreciate the film even further. I dont necessarily see anything wrong with it, it didnt overly intellectualise the film or anything
  - I agree but to me it's even much more impressive when you realize the movie is not just a random surrealist/impressionistc sequence of weird scenes; that actually, it all fits together into a coherent hole once you are able to stitch the pieces together.
4. I think I know the answer but the obvious question is if you've ever seen a David Lynch film before. He generally operates in the realm of surrealism, and his films are really things intended to be felt more than understood. Mulholland Drive is very open to interpretation, but the most important plot point to understand is that the first two thirds of the film are a dream, and when the cowboy says "Time to wake up, pretty girl", most if not all things after that are "real". The film fucking rules, I remember when I first saw it, I watched it like 20 times that month to try and figure out what its deal was.

Also some context for the film, it was originally intended to be a TV show. The initial concepts were for a Twin Peaks spin-off focused around the character Audrey, before becoming its own thing. The first two thirds of the film are effectively a pilot, but the TV studios rejected it. Lynch then got funding from Studio Canal to complete the work as a single feature film, so that also explains some of the disjointedness and plot threads that don't actually go anywhere
